The people of Westminster deserve the best from their council. Safe streets, clean neighbourhoods, a transport network that serves everyone, and public spaces that make Westminster one of the finest cities in the world.

However, when we talk to residents across Abbey Road ward, the same concerns come up time and again. Residents tell us that the streets are not as clean as they used to be, with persistent fly-tipping around communal waste areas like on Aberdeen Place and Boundary Road. Last autumn, leaves were left piling up on streets for days at a time, causing slip hazards for many of our elderly residents – especially on Loudon Road. Anti-social behaviour and noise complaints are often ignored by the council, for many weeks in some cases. The escalator in St John’s Wood tube has been out of action since early December.

And in November, over 1,000 local people signed our petition opposing the designs Labour have put forward for the C51 cycle highway which will run along Hamilton Terrace. Despite this overwhelming local opposition, the Labour administration intend to push ahead. A Conservative administration would scrap this costly scheme immediately, saving 130 parking spaces and much-needed EV charging points while preserving this beautiful tree-lined road.

It is clear that Labour are not listening to local people and this must change.

Our plan for Abbey Road is built around what residents have told us matters most to them: safe streets with visible enforcement, clean and well-maintained public spaces, and every pound of council tax spent on the services that matter. No more waste, no more vanity projects, no more reckless spending.

On Thursday 7th May, you have the chance to vote for real change in Westminster. We are asking for your trust and your vote to deliver the city you deserve.

Festus Akinbusoye, Cllr Hannah Galley, and Cllr Caroline Sargent
Your local Conservative candidates for Westminster's Abbey Road ward

Reform UK City of Westminster’s Nine Point Plan
1. Get Tough on Crime and Anti-Social Behaviour– The Conservatives cut CCTV when they were last in power, and closed our police stations. Labour Mayor Sadiq Khan has presided over a crime wave. Only Reform will fight crime by increasing frontline officer strength and adding CCTV coverage to keep you safe.
2. Cleaner, Tidier Streets – Westminster streets are dirtier than ever, with litter and dumping a regular feature. We will restore a Grade A street cleaning service as well as ensuring that dumpers are prosecuted, and e-bikes removed and their operators fined when parked badly. Graffiti will also be prioritised for immediate removal.
3. Co-ordinated Roadworks – Westminster roadworks are out of control, with journeys being disrupted daily because of the chaos of multiple schemes at the same time and roads repeatedly being re-dug. We will co-ordinate roadworks with AI to minimise disruption to your lives.
4. Cycleways That Work – We all want to see more cycling in Westminster but Conservative and Labour administrations have undertaken a war on pedestrians, disabled people and drivers through aggressive cycleway projects that make it difficult to cross roads, use cars or park. We will ensure that those who live in Westminster get the final say on future schemes so they work for all our road users..
5. Cut Waste and Save Money – Reform will promote value for money by targeting the millions wasted every year on pet ideological projects, weak major contracts, illegal migration and diversity schemes that prioritise social engineering over efficiency. Your Council Tax will stay low. Remember Labour lost £27 million of taxpayer’s money on a building company that went bust, and the Conservatives wasted £6 million on the failed Marble Arch Mound. Neither can be trusted with your money.
6. Oxford Street – We will oppose Mayor Khan’s plans which threaten congestion and mass disruption for local people and those looking to travel b

Born West London and living locally to Abbey Road her whole life, Esme has a deep love for the area and the people who make it what it is. With a degree in Sociology, she wants to bring an ethical, empathetic and human-first approach to local politics, without division.

Esme believes that for a community to truly flourish, its foundations must be solid –affordable housing, safe streets, reliable and accessible transport, flourishing green spaces and accessible culture.

As a parent, she is especially passionate about ensuring young people in the area are thriving – from safe journeys home from school to inclusive activities and youth services.

In a highly polarised (and toxic) political environment I believe there's a silent majority looking towards the centre, and for them the Liberal Democrat party is the natural home. But hey, this local election is all about local issues like crime, e-bikes and potholes. Kemi Badenoch says Lib Dems are only good for fixing the church roof. She's right, that's why there are more English councils controlled by the Lib Dems than the Conservatives.

The people of Westminster deserve the best from their council.

But people across Bayswater and Lancaster Gate have told us that they do not feel as safe as they used to. Streets are not as clean as they should be. Costs are rising while standards fall. And too often, it feels like the Labour-led council is simply not listening.

That must change. And you can help change it.

We are standing as your local Conservative candidates in May because Westminster deserves a council that keeps your city safe, looks after your streets, spends your money wisely, and listens to local people.

Our plan has been shaped by what you have told us matters most: safe streets, clean public spaces, and your money spent wisely.

No more excuses, waste, or reckless spending.

We have the plan, the team and the commitment to deliver it. But we need your support to make it happen.

On Thursday 7th May, you have the chance to vote for real change in Westminster. We are asking for your trust and your vote to deliver the Westminster you deserve.

Christabel Flight, Emily Sheffield, and Mark Tozer
Your local Conservative candidates for Westminster's Bayswater ward

She wants fairness in Westminer - Council and contractors’ staff to be paid the London Living Wage, more apprenticeships for Westminster residents, and protection for EU Citizens.

Max Sullivan is originally from Dublin, and works locally in marketing. He wants young people to have the best possible start in life, and is concerned about the damaging effects of Conservative cuts to children’s centres and youth clubs as well as to housing and health services for young people, including mental health provision. He is appalled by the Tories' financial mismanagement including the £6M Marble Arch Mound vanity project, and their lack of ambition on climate change and the environment.

Tim lives in the Ward having first moved to Bayswater in 1999. He had a long senior corporate career in commercial roles before recently spending more time volunteering and acting as a Non-Executive Director in the NHS and with housing charities.

1. Will increase the number of Police in the Streets in Westminster Council in particular in High streets and crime prone areas,will deploy CCTV in areas where women feel unsafe, will completely eradicate drug trafficking and car thefts by making Police answerable

2. Will prioritise Locals on all Council Benefits and will work hard for their rights

3.Will close money laundering fake Businesses in the Westminster high streets and will encourage locals to start new ventures

4. Will run surgery every week in the area and will be easily accessible for the voters to address their issues on a timely manner

Zubin has lived a few hundred metres from Church Street for his whole life, and has always loved the vibrancy of the area and its many food stalls, shops, and arts spaces. He joined the Green Party because of its support for environmental protection and immigrants’ rights, and its approach to reducing socio-economic inequality. Having helped to deliver sporting and academic youth programmes for several years, he is particularly keen to ensure that young people in Church Street have opportunities to develop their skills and engage with their community, which can also help to reduce rates of anti-social behaviour and crime.

People in Westminster deserve a council that works well, listens carefully, and gets the basics right.

In Church Street, residents have told us they want to feel safer, see cleaner streets, and know that their local area is being looked after properly. They want services that work, spending that makes sense, and a council that pays attention to what local people are saying.

That is what we want to help deliver.

We are standing in May because we believe Westminster should be a place where people feel proud of their neighbourhood, confident in their streets, and clear that their concerns are being heard.

Our priorities are simple: safer streets, cleaner public spaces, and better value for local taxpayers.

We want to bring a more practical, responsive approach to Westminster Council, focused on the things that matter most to residents.

On Thursday 7th May, you have the chance to choose the future direction of Westminster. We would be grateful for your support.

Natalia Awasty, Ahmed Imtiaz, and Magdalena Krzyzanowska
Your local Conservative candidates for Westminster's Church Street ward

Kirsty has worked for two prominent Liberal Democrats, Lynne Featherstone and Norman Lamb. Having seen the difficulties faced by many constituents on a daily basis, Kirsty was motivated to become a candidate in order to stand up for people’s rights, particularly those of under-represented women.

After working in retail and design for three years, Kirsty attended the University of London, Royal Holloway where she gained a BA (Hons) and MA in History. As a former mature student, she is passionate about making sure everyone can afford to have access to education. Kirsty further developed her interest in politics and liberalism at university and became an active member of the Liberal Democrats after starting to work for Lynne.

Kirsty is a strong advocate for penal reform and women’s rights, and works to promote access to politics for people from all walks of life.

Sarah's Campaign Priorities

Investing in the NHS and social care, and ensuring that frontline workers are paid properly and protected.

Boosting the local economy and creating green jobs, by supporting small businesses, innovation, and renewable energy.

Improving the quality and affordability of education, from early years to lifelong learning, and reducing the attainment gap.

Protecting the environment and tackling the climate emergency, by reducing emissions, promoting public transport, and enhancing green spaces.

Strengthening the rights and opportunities of individuals, and combating discrimination and hate crime.

Reforming the political system and restoring trust in democracy, by introducing proportional representation and devolving power to local communities.

I’ve been selected to stand as a Green Party candidate in Harrow Road, where I live close by and have been a committed local campaigner. I’m standing to be a bold voice for social and economic justice, climate action, and a fairer London for everyone. As an ecologist, I’ve worked hands-on to protect our rivers and green spaces, and I’ve campaigned for LGBTIQA+ rights, racial justice, and in solidarity with Palestine.

If elected, I’ll be an independent voice on the council answerable to residents, not party whips. I’ll fight for affordable housing, cleaner air, and public services that work for our community. When I’m not campaigning, I’m out in nature, involved in local organising, or at community events across the borough.

From the Harrow Road corridor to the wider neighbourhood, residents deserve streets that feel safe, clean, and well looked after.

People here know the importance of strong local services, reliable street cleaning, and a council that pays attention to the realities of daily life. Whether it is around the Grand Union Canal, local shopping parades, or the homes and estates that make up the ward, residents want to feel that their area is being properly cared for.

That is the standard we want to bring.

We believe Westminster should be a place where local people are listened to, public spaces are kept in good order, and taxpayers’ money is spent wisely.

Our focus is on the basics that matter most: safe streets, clean neighbourhoods, and better value for money.

On Thursday 7th May, you have the chance to back a more practical approach for Harrow Road and for Westminster.

Alexander Hawthorn, Iheoma Oteh, and Samuel Parr

1. Crime and ASB: “What will you do to address crime and deal with anti-social behaviour on the Hyde Park Estate? Do you have any plans for increased CCTV coverage?”

Westminster Council plans to install 350 new CCTV cameras by 2030 and we would work alongside the council to ensure this happens. 200 have already been delivered. 
Residents tell us that the biggest issue is not the lack of CCTV but the lack of follow‑up when problems are reported. We want the Council to work with the police so residents actually get responses, not reference numbers.
We support practical measures that make people feel safe, including visible community policing.
We also want investment in youth services and community programmes, because prevention is always more effective than enforcement alone.

Please see below an example of a Green Party Councillor tackling crime in Bristol:

In 2023, Green councillor Mohamed Makawi, whose friend had been fatally stabbed in Castle Park, proposed a motion to bring down knife crime in Bristol that was passed by full Council.

Rather than simply calling for more policing, the Green motion outlined practical solutions and addressed the social factors underlying knife violence.

It introduced simple but vital steps to save lives, such as providing emergency bleed kits and staff training for night-time venues, and providing more lighting and CCTV to fix ‘blind spots’ in areas of high knife crime.

2. Planning (double-glazing): “Many houses on the Hyde Park Estate are unable to install double-glazing due to planning constraints. This results in increased noise pollution and poor energy efficiency. What can you do rectify or mitigate this?”

We want planning rules to allow residents to live in warm, quiet, energy‑efficient homes.
We will push the Council to adopt modern, conservation‑grade double‑glazing and secondary‑glazing solutions that are already permitted in other conservation areas.
Improving insulation and reducing energy waste is essential for both

I’ve been your local councillor since 2022 in Lancaster Gate. As well as being your local representative, I’ve led the council’s nationally recognised house building programme and our work to protect private renters across the city, including more than doubling the size of the private renters enforcement team.

I want to create a fairer Pimlico. My key policies include:

1) Keeping Council Tax low. Westminster's Labour Council has the 2nd lowest Council Tax in the UK.

2) More affordable housing and greater rights for tenants and leaseholders.

3) Safe streets - on tackling anti-social behaviour we need greater preventative measures (e.g. funding for youth services) and deterrents. The Labour Council has installed 200 new CCTV cameras and we plan to increase this to 350 by 2028.

4) Clean Streets - I want to protect and enhance Westminster's renowned waste collection and street cleaning services.

On 7th May you will have a choice about the future of Pimlico. Continued neglect under Labour, or action with the Conservatives.

We’ve all seen Pimlico deteriorate. The streets are messier, crime is up, our tube station is often broken or shut, Pimlico Proms cancelled, and repairs take forever. We’ve also seen a lack of investment into St George’s Square Dog Park and Gardens, and a failure to deliver the renovation of Pimlico Gardens.

This is before you get to the decade of destruction set to be caused by Labour’s plan for the Pimlico District Heating Undertaking (PDHU).

It doesn’t have to be like this, and the Conservatives have a plan to get the Council working for you again.

We will invest in more street cleaning and create a Cabinet Member for Enforcement to ensure those who break the rules are caught. We will actually stand up to Sadiq Khan over crime and cuts to our transport links. We will fund and restore the Pimlico Proms. We will deliver the improvements to Pimlico Gardens, and we will fix the dog park.

Finally, we will scrap Labour’s PDHU plans, go back to the drawing board, consult properly with residents, and implement a block-by-block approach.

Recent polls show it is going to be really close here in Pimlico South, meaning if the Conservatives win here, we will most likely win back the council. So, on May 7th, the power really is in your hands.

Vote Conservative and Protect Pimlico.

Murad Gassanly, Roberto Salvia, and James Spencer

2025 Statement (Vincent Square)
My name is Nick Lockett and moved to London over 30 years ago and live in Westminster with my Chinese wife and our dog Chewey. 
My professional background was firstly in Finance and then in Law, firstly as a barrister and then as a solicitor-advocate, I run a firm of solicitors that is business orientated and a Trustee and Founder of a computer charity that helps underprivileged children to learn computer skills and having specialised in technology law for 30 years am a well-known AI expert. 
I have been an educational adviser and understand the issues of neurodiversity, having family members on the spectrum.
 I have been involved in many legal cases involving housing, homelessness, and violence against women and have also been a prosecutor for the CPS and I have prosecuted drug dealers and criminals.
I am a local resident in Westminster & I know what a great place this is to live and raise a family, but also how the area has been let down by the Labour-Conservative leads in Westminster Council and how homelessness, crime and drugs have exploded in Westminster in the last few years. 
Like many Westminster residents, I have watched with increasing frustration and anger, how our neighbourhood has become overrun with crime & drug dealers dealing openly on street corners, drug users using drugs on our doorsteps and leaving needles in the park, the increased homelessness caused by a tsunami of illegal migrants taking up the homeless shelters and council accommodation to the detriment of the existing homeless and defaecating openly in our streets and parks.
I've also been following the PDHU proposals with alarm as although not affected myself, I have many friends affected by it and the poor standards of the Council's maintanance and repairs.

The people of Westminster deserve the best from their council. Safe streets, clean neighbourhoods, assistance for those most in need, and public spaces that make Westminster one of the finest cities in the world.

We have the plan, the team and the commitment to deliver it. But we need your support to make it happen.

On Thursday, 7th May, you have the chance to vote for real change in Westminster. We are asking for your trust and your vote to deliver the city you deserve.

Born in Newcastle-upon-Tyne, we moved to Manchester when I was six and to London when twelve. Westminster has been my home since 1978. My degrees are BA Econ Hons (University of Manchester) and MSc (London School of Economics). My professional background primarily revolves around financial management in both industry and the charity sector. I dedicated a significant portion of time to caring for my elderly parents who were Holocaust survivors, which profoundly shaped my values and volunteering/political activities.

Prior to joining the party and simultaneously, I worked on hunger-related issues, and campaigned, since 1982, for peace and human rights in Israel-Palestine with Jewish and joint Jewish-Palestinian groups, including Jewish-Arab Dialogue and Jews for Justice for Palestinians.

After joining in 1997, I played a pivotal role in establishing West Central London Green Party in time for the first London Assembly elections, being active until 2002 when I concentrated on Israeli-Palestinian issues until 2017. In my role as Chair of Jewish Greens, I have been spending quite some time recently – following the Hamas attacks on October 7th and the subsequent Israeli attacks on Gaza – on this and related issues.

My other political foci are a Green New Deal; Universal Basic Income which would help resolve poverty and homelessness, and improve mental health; and the appreciation for diversity in our communities.

Beyond politics, I volunteered at Crisis at Christmas, enjoy exercising and shopping on my tricycle, tending to my plants, and reading; and am staff to two cats.

Published and promoted by Vivien Lichtenstein on behalf of the Green Party, c/o Upper Parts, 230 Kilburn Lane, London W10 4BA
I work locally as an NHS psychiatrist. I am passionate about addressing the underlying environmental causes of mental illness, making our streets safer and improving air quality.

Residents of St. James’s ward deserve a council that gets the basics right: safe streets, clean neighbourhoods, transport that works for everyone, and public spaces that make Westminster a place we can all be proud of.

But when we speak with local people, we hear the same message: too many people feel less safe than they used to, police presence is too thin on the ground, and our streets are being left looking tired. Costs are rising, yet standards are slipping, and the Labour council simply is not listening despite seemingly endless consultations.

That isn’t good enough, and it doesn’t have to be this way. That’s why we’re standing as your local Conservative candidates this May: to bring a sharper focus on safety, cleaner streets, and a council that treats your money with respect.

Our priorities are simple, and they come straight from what you’ve told us:

Safer streets, with visible local enforcement and action on anti-social behaviour.
Cleaner neighbourhoods and better-kept public spaces every day, not just for special occasions.
Value for money: every pound of council tax is focused on frontline services, not waste.
We’re ready to get to work, and we’ll keep listening all year round, not just at election time. If you back us, we’ll fight for the St. James’s you expect and deserve.

On Thursday, 7th May, please vote Conservative for real change in Westminster.

This is Westminster. We deserve better.

The people of Westminster deserve the best from their council. Safe streets, clean neighbourhoods, and public spaces make this one of the finest parts of London to live in.

But when we speak to residents across Vincent Square Ward, the same concerns come up time and again. Too many people tell us they don’t feel as safe as they used to. Streets are not as clean as they should be. Rough sleeping has become more visible, with too many people not getting the help they need. And too often, it feels like the Council simply isn’t listening.

That must change.

We are standing to serve Vincent Square Ward because Westminster deserves better. A council that keeps streets safe, looks after the area properly, supports people off the streets, spends your money wisely, and listens to local people.

Our plan is built around what residents tell us matters most: safer streets with visible enforcement, clean and well-maintained public spaces, and proper support to help people move off the streets for good, all while ensuring responsible use of council tax.

We all live here in the ward, and between us bring over 25 years of public service. This is our community, and we care deeply about getting this right.

We have the team and the commitment to deliver. But we need your support. On Thursday, 7th May, we ask you to re-elect Selina Short and David Harvey, and elect Ben Sewell, to serve as your local councillors.

Cllr David Harvey, Ben Sewell, and Cllr Selina Short

I am a local resident in Pimlico and, like you, know what a great place this is to live and raise a family. My professional background is in policy development and tackling violence against women and girls. I’m the Head of Public Affairs and Policy for a national domestic abuse charity. This follows on from serving two terms as a Labour Councillor in West London, where I was the Scrutiny Chair for the Council’s policy on Violence Against Women and Girls and then Cabinet Member for Community Safety.

I care deeply about the issues affecting Vincent Square, particularly antisocial behaviour, drug dealing and challenges with housing repairs. It’s been great to speak to so many residents already and start getting issues reported and sorted.

My priorities will be to work with the Council to introduce measures to tackle antisocial behaviour and increase women’s safety, improve housing repair services in the area and stand up for private renters dealing with rogue landlords.

I’ll be a hard-working and accessible local Councillor for Vincent Square Ward. I’ll keep in touch with residents on the doorstep, through regular advice surgeries and at community events, taking up your concerns and getting things done.

Andrew has lived in the Vincent Square ward for 27 years. His family can be traced back to Westminster in the 1910.
 
He has experience gained in the NHS, the territorial armed services and in practice/industry. Andrew has given many years' service to Law Centre/Advice Service organisations where he has represented and won many successful outcomes for hundreds of clients with their housing, social welfare, and community support needs. Clients include families, persons under disabilities, children and victims of domestic abuse.
 
He currently operates a family rental property business.
 
Andrew will apply his professional and life skills to deliver:
 
1.      The Reform UK pledges in Vincent Square/Westminster;
 
2.      Working within Westminster to minimise the cost of living crisis for all;
 
3.      Supporting law and order policies throughout Westminster, focusing on local crime issues; and
 
4.      Prioritising British nationals for community services and housing.

Tim Lord is a Government Affairs and Regulatory Director with over twenty years' experience in his field. Cambridge-educated, Tim trained as a solicitor at Linklaters before joining the telecommunications sector in the early days of cable and internet.

In 2017, he stood as an independent candidate in Westminster and wants MPs to listen to their constituents.

Why get active in politics now?

"Through my work in telecommunications, broadcasting and the internet, I understand negotiation and rational decision making. In my work, I’ve brought businesses to new markets and dealt with the European Commission. The extensive legal cases I’ve led before the UK competition regulators had put my teams in the frontline with Ofcom, the Office of Fair Trading and the Competition Commission.”

“My work is all about negotiation, it’s clear to me that the government’s preparations for the negotiations are inadequate, and its conduct of them is seriously misguided.” Tim explains.

Can you help me keep on working for Westbourne?

As a local councillor I have helped hundreds of individual constituents, and worked with Westbourne Neighbourhood Forum, the Safer Neighbourhood team, and the North Paddington Programme to help them work properly for the local community. I help estate and street residents, local schools and churches solve their problems with the council and others.

As a Labour council for the first time, Westminster has built council homes like 300 Harrow Road and Westmead, opened community venues like Brunel Happy Hub and The Exchange, and supported local venues like Paddington Arts and Grand Junction.

If we're re-elected we'll work to make Westbourne safer and more pleasant with improved street lighting, more CCTV, more City Inspectors and improvement to local green spaces. We'll be able to improve housing repairs by improving the housing contracts the Tories tied us in to, and we'll keep council tax down.

Westbourne combines busy streets, local homes, and a real sense of neighbourhood life, and residents deserve a council that reflects that.

People here want to see well-kept streets, safer routes through the area, and a council that understands the pressures on local families, shops, and community spaces. Around Westbourne Grove, the local station, and the streets that connect this part of Westminster, residents expect visible care and real attention to detail.

That is what we want to deliver.

We believe Westminster should be a place where local people feel heard, public spaces are looked after properly, and money is spent with care.

Our priorities are clear: safer streets, cleaner neighbourhoods, and better value for residents.

On Thursday, 7th May, you have the chance to choose a more practical and responsive approach for Westbourne and for Westminster.
